findings.description: Expiry check is inverted: 'age < self.ttl' returns True for fresh entries, so _is_expired reports the opposite of reality.
Due to the inverted check, fresh L1 entries are deleted while genuinely expired entries fall through and are served as hits, so TTL eviction behaves backwards.
pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis enables arbitrary code execution if an attacker can write to the cache backend.
Using pickle.dumps as the cache serialization format is the root cause of the unsafe deserialization risk; a safe codec (e.g. JSON/msgpack) should be used for untrusted stores.
get returns None for both a cache miss and a legitimately cached None, so get_or_compute recomputes every time a None value is stored (cache penetration).
The L1 _local dict has no size bound or LRU eviction, so it can grow without limit and leak memory.
No synchronization around _local mutations (get/set/invalidate); concurrent access can raise KeyError on del or corrupt stats in a threaded context.
The fixture RedisClient.setex ignores ttl_seconds, so L2 entries never expire and the two tiers can diverge on freshness.
stats.local_entries is only updated in set(), not when get() populates _local from Redis, so the stored field drifts from the real entry count.
Module-level logger is defined but never used anywhere in the file (dead code).
Inconsistent deletion idiom: del self._local[key] here versus self._local.pop(key, None) in invalidate.
